answer:
Comparing Two Approaches in Utility Types with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Utility Types are built-in TypeScript constructs that transform existing types into new ones without redefining them from scratch. The two primary approaches are: Approach A: Composed Built-in Utility Types (chaining Pick, Omit, Partial, Required, Record) and Approach B: Custom Mapped Types with Conditional Logic (using keyof, in, extends, infer to build bespoke transformations). Approach A is readable and familiar to any TypeScript developer; Approach B provides maximum flexibility for complex domain constraints but has a steeper learning curve and can produce opaque compiler errors.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the LeaveRequest entity has 14 fields. Different API operations need different subsets: the creation endpoint omits id, createdAt, and status; the update endpoint makes most fields optional; the approval endpoint only needs id and status. Manoj evaluated both approaches:

Approach A (chosen for most cases): Omit<LeaveRequest, 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'status'> for creation DTO, Partial<Pick<LeaveRequest, 'startDate' | 'endDate' | 'reason'>> for update DTO. Clean, readable, and every team member understood it instantly.

Approach B (chosen for the audit log): The audit system needed a type that marked every field as readonly and appended an _old suffixed version. Built-in utility types cannot rename keys, so Manoj wrote a custom mapped type: type AuditSnapshot<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T as K]: T[K] } & { readonly [K in keyof T as `${string & K}_old`]: T[K] }.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// === Approach A: Composed Built-in Utility Types ===

interface LeaveRequest {
  id: string;
  employeeId: string;
  startDate: Date;
  endDate: Date;
  type: 'casual' | 'sick' | 'earned';
  reason: string;
  status: 'submitted' | 'approved' | 'rejected';
  days: number;
  createdAt: Date;
  updatedAt: Date;
}

// Creation DTO — omit server-generated fields
type CreateLeaveDTO = Omit<LeaveRequest, 'id' | 'status' | 'days' | 'createdAt' | 'updatedAt'>;

// Update DTO — only editable fields, all optional
type UpdateLeaveDTO = Partial<Pick<LeaveRequest, 'startDate' | 'endDate' | 'reason'>>;

// Approval DTO — minimal
type ApprovalDTO = Pick<LeaveRequest, 'id' | 'status'>;

// List response — include computed display name
type LeaveListItem = Pick<LeaveRequest, 'id' | 'startDate' | 'endDate' | 'type' | 'status' | 'days'>
  & { employeeName: string };


// === Approach B: Custom Mapped Type (for audit snapshot) ===

// Creates readonly snapshot with _old suffixed copies of each field
type AuditSnapshot<T> = {
  readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K];
} & {
  readonly [K in keyof T as `${string & K}_old`]: T[K];
};

// Usage: tracks before & after for every leave field
type LeaveAuditEntry = AuditSnapshot<Pick<LeaveRequest, 'status' | 'startDate' | 'endDate'>>;
// Result: { readonly status, readonly startDate, readonly endDate,
//           readonly status_old, readonly startDate_old, readonly endDate_old }

// Conditional utility — make only Date fields optional (custom logic)
type DatesOptional<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]: T[K] extends Date ? T[K] | undefined : T[K];
};

type FlexibleLeave = DatesOptional<CreateLeaveDTO>;
// startDate and endDate become optional; type, reason, employeeId stay required


// === Fastify route using the composed DTOs ===
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';

async function leaveRoutes(app: FastifyInstance) {
  app.post<{ Body: CreateLeaveDTO }>('/api/leave', async (req, reply) => {
    const dto: CreateLeaveDTO = req.body;
    // TypeScript enforces: dto has no id, status, createdAt
    const leave = await createLeave(dto);
    return reply.code(201).send(leave);
  });

  app.patch<{ Body: UpdateLeaveDTO; Params: { id: string } }>(
    '/api/leave/:id',
    async (req, reply) => {
      // All fields optional — only update what's provided
      const updated = await updateLeave(req.params.id, req.body);
      return reply.send(updated);
    }
  );
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

While building the HR Leave System's API, Manoj initially defined separate interfaces for each DTO manually: CreateLeaveDTO, UpdateLeaveDTO, etc. When a new field attachmentUrl was added to LeaveRequest, he forgot to add it to the creation DTO. The bug only surfaced in production when attachments silently disappeared during leave creation because the Fastify schema validation stripped the unrecognized field.

Manoj refactored all DTOs to derive from the base LeaveRequest interface using Omit and Pick. Now when a field is added to the base interface, all derived types automatically include or exclude it based on their composition. He caught the pattern by running tsc --noEmit after adding a field — TypeScript immediately flagged places where the new field was expected but missing. This “single source of truth” approach eliminated an entire class of drift bugs.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
DTO definitions drift from base entity when written manuallyAlways derive DTOs from the base interface using Omit, Pick, Partial; never duplicate field lists
Deeply nested utility type compositions produce unreadable hover tooltipsUse intermediate type aliases with descriptive names; add JSDoc comments explaining the transformation intent
Custom mapped types with template literal keys break autocomplete in some IDEsTest IDE support before adopting; keep template literal mapped types in a single utility file with explicit tests
Partial makes ALL fields optional, including ones that should stay requiredUse Partial<Pick<T, K>> & Required<Pick<T, M>> to selectively make fields optional while keeping others required

7. Validation Strategy

  • Compile-time check: Add a CI step running tsc --noEmit — ensures all DTO derivations resolve correctly when base types change
  • Type-level test: Use type AssertEquals<T, U> = T extends U ? U extends T ? true : false : false to verify DTO shapes match expectations in test files
  • Runtime validation alignment: Generate Fastify JSON schemas from the TypeScript DTOs using typescript-json-schema so runtime validation matches compile-time types
  • Snapshot test: Serialize resolved DTO types to a snapshot; CI fails if a base type change unexpectedly modifies a DTO shape

Designing a Practical Implementation Using Utility Types for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

Utility Types in a mid-size product serve as the type-level glue between your domain entities, API contracts, form states, and database schemas. A well-designed utility type system ensures that when a domain entity changes, all derived types (DTOs, form types, API responses, validation schemas) update automatically. The goal is a single source of truth at the type level: one base interface per entity, with all variations derived using utility types.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the core Deal entity had 18 fields spanning creation, pipeline stage tracking, revenue calculations, and audit timestamps. The Angular 18 frontend, Express.js API, and MongoDB layer each needed different projections of this entity. Manoj designed a utility type architecture where the base Deal interface lived in a shared types/ package, and every layer derived its types from it — the frontend form type used Omit + Partial, the API response used Pick + extensions, and the MongoDB document type added _id via intersection.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// shared/types/deal.ts — Single source of truth

export interface Deal {
  id: string;
  companyName: string;
  contactEmail: string;
  contactPhone: string;
  stage: 'lead' | 'qualified' | 'proposal' | 'negotiation' | 'closed-won' | 'closed-lost';
  revenue: number;
  probability: number;
  assignedTo: string;
  notes: string;
  source: 'website' | 'referral' | 'cold-call' | 'event';
  nextFollowUp: Date | null;
  tags: string[];
  createdAt: Date;
  updatedAt: Date;
  createdBy: string;
  lastModifiedBy: string;
  closedAt: Date | null;
  lostReason: string | null;
}

// Server-generated fields that clients never send
type ServerFields = 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'updatedAt' | 'createdBy' | 'lastModifiedBy';

// ---- API DTOs ----
export type CreateDealDTO = Omit<Deal, ServerFields | 'closedAt' | 'lostReason'>;

export type UpdateDealDTO = Partial<Pick<Deal,
  'companyName' | 'contactEmail' | 'contactPhone' | 'stage' |
  'revenue' | 'probability' | 'notes' | 'nextFollowUp' | 'tags'
>>;

export type DealStageUpdate = Pick<Deal, 'id' | 'stage'> & {
  revenue?: number;       // Can update revenue when changing stage
  lostReason?: string;    // Required only when stage is closed-lost
};

// ---- Frontend types ----
export type DealListItem = Pick<Deal,
  'id' | 'companyName' | 'stage' | 'revenue' | 'probability' | 'assignedTo' | 'nextFollowUp'
>;

export type DealFormState = CreateDealDTO & {
  errors: Partial<Record<keyof CreateDealDTO, string>>;
  isDirty: boolean;
  isSubmitting: boolean;
};

// Recharts-compatible shape for pipeline chart
export type DealChartData = Pick<Deal, 'stage' | 'revenue'> & {
  count: number;
  fill: string;
};

// ---- Database types ----
import { ObjectId } from 'mongodb';

export type DealDocument = Omit<Deal, 'id'> & {
  _id: ObjectId;
  __v: number;
};

// Conversion utility
export function toDealFromDoc(doc: DealDocument): Deal {
  const { _id, __v, ...rest } = doc;
  return { ...rest, id: _id.toHexString() };
}

// ---- Advanced: Conditional utility for nullable fields ----
type NullableKeys<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]: null extends T[K] ? K : never;
}[keyof T];

type RequiredForClose = Required<Pick<Deal, NullableKeys<Deal>>>;
// Forces closedAt and lostReason to be non-null
// Used when finalizing a deal: all nullable fields must be filled


// ---- Express route using these types ----
import { Router, Request, Response } from 'express';

const router = Router();

router.post('/api/deals', async (req: Request<{}, {}, CreateDealDTO>, res: Response) => {
  const dto: CreateDealDTO = req.body;
  // TypeScript ensures dto has no id, createdAt, updatedAt, etc.
  const deal = await dealService.create(dto, req.user!.id);
  res.status(201).json(deal);
});

router.get('/api/deals', async (req: Request, res: Response<DealListItem[]>) => {
  const deals: DealListItem[] = await dealService.list(req.query);
  // Response is typed — cannot accidentally include fields not in DealListItem
  res.json(deals);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj encountered a subtle bug where the Recharts pipeline funnel chart crashed with NaN values. The chart component expected DealChartData with numeric revenue, but the MongoDB aggregation pipeline returned revenue as a string when the sum was zero (Mongo's $sum on an empty group returns 0 but the Express serialization layer converted it).

The root cause was that the DealChartData type said revenue: number but runtime data violated this contract. Manoj fixed it by adding a Zod schema derived from the same base type: const DealChartDataSchema = z.object({ stage: z.string(), revenue: z.number(), count: z.number(), fill: z.string() }). The parse step DealChartDataSchema.parse(data) caught the type mismatch at the API boundary before it reached the chart. The key learning: TypeScript types vanish at runtime; always validate at system boundaries.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Adding a field to the base entity silently appears in DTOs that should exclude itUse explicit Pick for DTOs that should only include specific fields rather than Omit for exclusion; Pick is additive-safe
Runtime data does not match TypeScript types (types are erased at compile time)Validate at API boundaries using Zod or Ajv schemas derived from or aligned with your TypeScript types
Deep nesting of utility types makes error messages unreadableCreate named intermediate types; use type Expand<T> = T extends infer U ? { [K in keyof U]: U[K] } : never to flatten for debugging
Shared types package becomes a deployment bottleneck (frontend and backend must update together)Version the shared types package; use semver and only bump major on breaking changes; keep the package minimal

7. Validation Strategy

  • Type coverage: Run tsc --noEmit --strict in CI; zero type errors means all derivations are consistent with the base
  • Schema alignment test: Auto-generate Zod schemas from TypeScript types using ts-to-zod; test that they parse sample data correctly
  • Field addition test: Add a test field to the base entity, run CI — verify no unexpected DTO includes it (use Pick-based DTOs)
  • API contract test: Supertest integration tests validate that POST/PATCH endpoints reject payloads with extra fields and accept valid ones matching the DTO shape

Comparing Two Approaches in Decorators with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Decorators are a TypeScript feature (Stage 3 proposal, enabled via experimentalDecorators or the TC39 standard syntax) that attach metadata or modify class declarations, methods, properties, and parameters at design time. The two approaches are: Approach A: Legacy/Experimental Decorators (TypeScript's experimentalDecorators + reflect-metadata, used by Angular, NestJS, TypeORM) and Approach B: TC39 Stage 3 Standard Decorators (TypeScript 5.0+, no reflect-metadata needed, uses the decorator context API). Approach A has massive ecosystem support but is technically non-standard; Approach B is standards-compliant but has limited library support as of 2025.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System built with Angular 18 and Fastify, decorators appeared in two contexts:

Approach A (Angular frontend): Angular relies entirely on experimental decorators — @Component, @Injectable, @Input, @Output. The leave request form component uses @Component({ selector: 'app-leave-form', ... }). Manoj had no choice here; Angular mandates experimental decorators.

Approach B (Fastify backend): On the server side, Manoj evaluated adding decorators for route authorization. With experimental decorators, he would use @Authorize('manager') on controller methods. With TC39 decorators, the same effect is achieved but with a different API. He chose a simpler middleware-based approach on the backend since Fastify's plugin system already handles cross-cutting concerns without needing class-based controllers, making decorators unnecessary overhead.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// === Approach A: Experimental Decorators (Angular Component) ===
// Requires: "experimentalDecorators": true, "emitDecoratorMetadata": true

import { Component, OnInit, inject } from '@angular/core';
import { FormBuilder, FormGroup, Validators } from '@angular/forms';
import { LeaveService } from '../../services/leave.service';

@Component({
  selector: 'app-leave-form',
  templateUrl: './leave-form.component.html',
  styleUrls: ['./leave-form.component.css'],
  standalone: true,
})
export class LeaveFormComponent implements OnInit {
  private fb = inject(FormBuilder);
  private leaveService = inject(LeaveService);
  leaveForm!: FormGroup;

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.leaveForm = this.fb.group({
      type: ['casual', Validators.required],
      startDate: ['', Validators.required],
      endDate: ['', Validators.required],
      reason: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(10)]],
    });
  }

  submit(): void {
    if (this.leaveForm.valid) {
      this.leaveService.applyLeave(this.leaveForm.value).subscribe();
    }
  }
}

// Custom experimental decorator for logging method execution time
function LogExecutionTime(
  target: any,
  propertyKey: string,
  descriptor: PropertyDescriptor
): PropertyDescriptor {
  const original = descriptor.value;
  descriptor.value = function (...args: any[]) {
    const start = performance.now();
    const result = original.apply(this, args);
    const end = performance.now();
    console.log(`${propertyKey} executed in ${(end - start).toFixed(2)}ms`);
    return result;
  };
  return descriptor;
}


// === Approach B: TC39 Standard Decorators (TypeScript 5.0+) ===
// No tsconfig flag needed for TC39 decorators in TS 5.0+

function logExecutionTime<T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
  originalMethod: T,
  context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
): T {
  const methodName = String(context.name);
  function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
    const start = performance.now();
    const result = originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
    const elapsed = performance.now() - start;
    console.log(`${methodName} executed in ${elapsed.toFixed(2)}ms`);
    return result;
  }
  return replacement as T;
}

// TC39 decorator for authorization (standalone Fastify controller)
function authorize(role: string) {
  return function <T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
    originalMethod: T,
    context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
  ): T {
    const methodName = String(context.name);
    function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
      const req = args[0]; // Fastify request
      if (req.user?.role !== role) {
        throw new Error(`Unauthorized: ${methodName} requires role '${role}'`);
      }
      return originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
    }
    return replacement as T;
  };
}

class LeaveController {
  @logExecutionTime
  @authorize('employee')
  async applyLeave(req: any, reply: any) {
    // Business logic here
    const leave = await createLeave(req.body);
    return reply.code(201).send(leave);
  }

  @logExecutionTime
  @authorize('manager')
  async approveLeave(req: any, reply: any) {
    const result = await approveLeave(req.params.id);
    return reply.send(result);
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

While building the HR Leave System's Angular frontend, Manoj created a custom @Debounce(300) experimental decorator for the leave search input. It worked in development but failed in production builds. The issue: Angular's AOT compiler processes decorators differently than JIT. The decorator modified the method descriptor at runtime, but AOT had already compiled the template bindings assuming the original method signature.

Manoj diagnosed it by comparing JIT vs AOT builds — the JIT build worked because decorators ran at class definition time before template binding. In AOT, the template was pre-compiled. The fix was to move the debounce logic into an RxJS debounceTime(300) pipe inside the component rather than using a method decorator. Lesson learned: in Angular, custom method decorators that change behavior (not just metadata) can conflict with AOT compilation. Metadata-only decorators like @Component are safe because Angular's compiler understands them natively.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Experimental and TC39 decorators cannot coexist in the same projectChoose one mode per project; Angular projects must use experimental; new standalone libraries can use TC39
Custom decorators that modify behavior can break Angular AOTIn Angular, only use decorators for metadata; move behavior modification into services, pipes, or RxJS operators
TC39 decorators lack parameter decorator supportUse dependency injection containers that work without parameter decorators (e.g., token-based injection); or stick with experimental if parameter decorators are critical
Decorator execution order is bottom-up (last decorator runs first)Document decorator order in code comments; write integration tests that verify combined decorator behavior

7. Validation Strategy

  • AOT build test: Run ng build --configuration production in CI to catch decorator issues that only appear in AOT mode
  • Decorator unit test: Test each custom decorator in isolation by applying it to a mock class and verifying the modified behavior
  • Execution order test: Stack multiple decorators on a test method, verify they execute in the expected order (bottom-up for experimental, same for TC39)
  • Type safety check: Ensure decorators preserve the method's TypeScript signature; use Parameters<typeof method> and ReturnType assertions in tests

Designing a Practical Implementation Using Decorators for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

In a mid-size product, decorators serve as a declarative way to attach cross-cutting concerns — authentication, validation, caching, logging, rate limiting — to class methods and properties without polluting business logic. A well-designed decorator system creates a vocabulary of reusable annotations (@Authorize, @Validate, @Cache, @Log) that developers compose on controller methods, keeping route handlers focused on business logic while infrastructure concerns are handled declaratively.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub application (React 18 frontend, Fastify backend), the API had 15+ endpoints for contact CRUD, image uploads via Cloudinary, and search. Each endpoint needed: JWT authentication, input validation, error handling, and response time logging. Initially, every route handler repeated the same boilerplate. Manoj refactored the backend into a class-based controller pattern with decorators to eliminate the repetition. Each decorator handled one concern, and route handlers became pure business logic.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// decorators/log.ts — Execution time + request logging
function Log<T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
  originalMethod: T,
  context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
): T {
  const name = String(context.name);
  async function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
    const req = args[0];
    const start = performance.now();
    console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${req.method} ${req.url} -> ${name}`);
    try {
      const result = await originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
      console.log(`[${name}] completed in ${(performance.now() - start).toFixed(1)}ms`);
      return result;
    } catch (err) {
      console.error(`[${name}] failed after ${(performance.now() - start).toFixed(1)}ms`, err);
      throw err;
    }
  }
  return replacement as T;
}

// decorators/authorize.ts — JWT role check
function Authorize(requiredRole: string) {
  return function <T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
    originalMethod: T,
    context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
  ): T {
    async function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
      const req = args[0];
      const reply = args[1];
      try {
        await req.jwtVerify();
      } catch {
        return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid or missing token' });
      }
      if (req.user.role !== requiredRole && req.user.role !== 'admin') {
        return reply.code(403).send({ error: `Requires role: ${requiredRole}` });
      }
      return originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
    }
    return replacement as T;
  };
}

// decorators/validate.ts — Zod schema validation
import { ZodSchema } from 'zod';

function Validate(schema: ZodSchema) {
  return function <T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
    originalMethod: T,
    context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
  ): T {
    async function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
      const req = args[0];
      const reply = args[1];
      const result = schema.safeParse(req.body);
      if (!result.success) {
        return reply.code(400).send({
          error: 'Validation failed',
          details: result.error.issues.map(i => ({
            field: i.path.join('.'),
            message: i.message,
          })),
        });
      }
      req.validatedBody = result.data;
      return originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
    }
    return replacement as T;
  };
}

// decorators/cache.ts — Simple in-memory cache
function Cache(ttlSeconds: number) {
  const store = new Map<string, { data: any; expiry: number }>();
  return function <T extends (...args: any[]) => any>(
    originalMethod: T,
    context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
  ): T {
    async function replacement(this: any, ...args: any[]) {
      const req = args[0];
      const key = `${String(context.name)}:${req.url}`;
      const cached = store.get(key);
      if (cached && cached.expiry > Date.now()) {
        return args[1].send(cached.data);
      }
      const result = await originalMethod.call(this, ...args);
      store.set(key, { data: result, expiry: Date.now() + ttlSeconds * 1000 });
      return result;
    }
    return replacement as T;
  };
}

// === ContactsHub Controller using composed decorators ===
import { z } from 'zod';

const CreateContactSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  email: z.string().email(),
  phone: z.string().optional(),
  company: z.string().optional(),
  avatarUrl: z.string().url().optional(),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
});

class ContactController {
  @Log
  @Authorize('user')
  @Validate(CreateContactSchema)
  async createContact(req: any, reply: any) {
    // Pure business logic — auth, validation, logging already handled
    const contact = await Contact.create({
      ...req.validatedBody,
      userId: req.user.id,
    });
    return reply.code(201).send(contact);
  }

  @Log
  @Authorize('user')
  @Cache(60)
  async listContacts(req: any, reply: any) {
    const contacts = await Contact.find({ userId: req.user.id })
      .sort({ updatedAt: -1 })
      .lean();
    return reply.send(contacts);
  }

  @Log
  @Authorize('user')
  async deleteContact(req: any, reply: any) {
    const contact = await Contact.findOneAndDelete({
      _id: req.params.id,
      userId: req.user.id,
    });
    if (!contact) return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'Not found' });
    // Clean up Cloudinary image
    if (contact.avatarUrl) {
      await cloudinary.uploader.destroy(extractPublicId(contact.avatarUrl));
    }
    return reply.code(204).send();
  }
}

// Register with Fastify
const controller = new ContactController();
app.post('/api/contacts', controller.createContact.bind(controller));
app.get('/api/contacts', controller.listContacts.bind(controller));
app.delete('/api/contacts/:id', controller.deleteContact.bind(controller));

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After implementing the decorator-based controller in ContactsHub, Manoj noticed that this was undefined inside decorated methods when registered as Fastify route handlers. The issue: Fastify calls route handlers as standalone functions, losing the class instance context. The decorators preserved this via originalMethod.call(this, ...args), but the outermost call from Fastify had no this binding.

Manoj traced it by adding console.log('this:', this) at the start of a handler — it printed undefined. The fix was straightforward: use .bind(controller) when registering routes, or use arrow function wrappers: app.post('/api/contacts', (req, reply) => controller.createContact(req, reply)). He standardized on .bind() and added a registration helper that auto-binds all methods. The takeaway: decorators modify method descriptors but cannot fix the this binding when the method is extracted from its class.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
this binding lost when class methods are used as route handlersAlways .bind(instance) when registering methods as callbacks; or use a route registration helper that auto-binds
Decorator execution order is unintuitive (bottom-up composition)Document the order explicitly; @Log @Authorize @Validate means validate runs first, then authorize, then log wraps everything
Async decorators can swallow errors if not properly awaitedAlways await originalMethod.call(this, ...args) in async decorators; wrap in try/catch to ensure errors propagate
Cache decorator returns stale data after mutationsImplement cache invalidation: clear the cache map entries for the affected URL prefix after any write operation (POST, PUT, DELETE)

7. Validation Strategy

  • Decorator isolation test: Apply each decorator to a mock handler, verify it adds the expected behavior (e.g., @Authorize returns 401 without token, 403 with wrong role, passes through with correct role)
  • Composition test: Stack all decorators on a test handler, verify they execute in the expected order by checking log output sequence
  • Binding test: Register decorated method as Fastify handler, verify this is correctly bound and instance properties are accessible
  • Playwright E2E: Test ContactsHub create/list/delete flows end-to-end, ensuring decorators don't break the request/response cycle

Comparing Two Approaches in Error Boundaries in TS with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Error Boundaries in TypeScript refer to structured patterns for catching, typing, and handling errors at system boundaries (API responses, async operations, component rendering). The two approaches are: Approach A: Try-Catch with Typed Error Unions (using discriminated unions like Result<T, E> or { success: true; data: T } | { success: false; error: E }) and Approach B: React Error Boundary Components + TypeScript generics (class components with componentDidCatch for UI error isolation, enhanced with TypeScript generics for typed fallback props). Approach A is framework-agnostic and works across frontend/backend; Approach B is React-specific but provides UI-level error containment that prevents one broken component from crashing the entire app.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React 18 + Fastify), errors occurred at multiple layers: the Fastify API could return 400/401/404/500, Cloudinary image uploads could timeout, MongoDB queries could fail, and React components could throw during rendering. Manoj used both approaches:

Approach A (backend + API calls): All Fastify route handlers returned typed Result<T, AppError> objects. The React frontend's API client transformed HTTP responses into the same Result type, so every fetch call returned either { success: true, data } or { success: false, error } — never an untyped thrown exception.

Approach B (React rendering): The contact list, contact detail, and image upload components were each wrapped in an ErrorBoundary component. When the contact detail view crashed (e.g., due to unexpected null data), only that panel showed a fallback UI; the sidebar contact list remained functional.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// === Approach A: Result<T, E> Pattern (framework-agnostic) ===

// types/result.ts — Typed error union
type AppError = {
  code: 'VALIDATION' | 'NOT_FOUND' | 'UNAUTHORIZED' | 'UPLOAD_FAILED' | 'INTERNAL';
  message: string;
  field?: string;
};

type Result<T> =
  | { success: true; data: T }
  | { success: false; error: AppError };

// Helper constructors
const ok = <T>(data: T): Result<T> => ({ success: true, data });
const fail = <T>(error: AppError): Result<T> => ({ success: false, error });

// Fastify route returning typed Result
async function createContact(req: any, reply: any): Promise<void> {
  const parsed = CreateContactSchema.safeParse(req.body);
  if (!parsed.success) {
    const result: Result<never> = fail({
      code: 'VALIDATION',
      message: parsed.error.issues[0].message,
      field: parsed.error.issues[0].path.join('.'),
    });
    return reply.code(400).send(result);
  }

  try {
    const contact = await Contact.create({ ...parsed.data, userId: req.user.id });
    const result: Result<typeof contact> = ok(contact);
    return reply.code(201).send(result);
  } catch (err) {
    const result: Result<never> = fail({ code: 'INTERNAL', message: 'Failed to create contact' });
    return reply.code(500).send(result);
  }
}

// React API client consuming Result
async function apiCreateContact(data: CreateContactDTO): Promise<Result<Contact>> {
  try {
    const res = await fetch('/api/contacts', {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify(data),
    });
    const json = await res.json();
    return json as Result<Contact>; // Server already returns Result shape
  } catch {
    return fail({ code: 'INTERNAL', message: 'Network error' });
  }
}

// Usage in React component — exhaustive handling
const handleSubmit = async (formData: CreateContactDTO) => {
  const result = await apiCreateContact(formData);
  if (result.success) {
    setContacts(prev => [result.data, ...prev]);
    toast.success('Contact created!');
  } else {
    switch (result.error.code) {
      case 'VALIDATION':
        setFieldError(result.error.field!, result.error.message);
        break;
      case 'UNAUTHORIZED':
        navigate('/login');
        break;
      default:
        toast.error(result.error.message);
    }
  }
};


// === Approach B: React Error Boundary with TypeScript ===

import React, { Component, ReactNode } from 'react';

interface ErrorBoundaryProps {
  fallback: ReactNode | ((error: Error, reset: () => void) => ReactNode);
  onError?: (error: Error, errorInfo: React.ErrorInfo) => void;
  children: ReactNode;
}

interface ErrorBoundaryState {
  hasError: boolean;
  error: Error | null;
}

class ErrorBoundary extends Component<ErrorBoundaryProps, ErrorBoundaryState> {
  state: ErrorBoundaryState = { hasError: false, error: null };

  static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): ErrorBoundaryState {
    return { hasError: true, error };
  }

  componentDidCatch(error: Error, errorInfo: React.ErrorInfo): void {
    console.error('ErrorBoundary caught:', error, errorInfo);
    this.props.onError?.(error, errorInfo);
  }

  reset = (): void => {
    this.setState({ hasError: false, error: null });
  };

  render(): ReactNode {
    if (this.state.hasError && this.state.error) {
      const { fallback } = this.props;
      if (typeof fallback === 'function') {
        return fallback(this.state.error, this.reset);
      }
      return fallback;
    }
    return this.props.children;
  }
}

// Usage in ContactsHub layout
function ContactsApp() {
  return (
    <div className="contacts-layout">
      <ErrorBoundary fallback={<p>Sidebar failed to load.</p>}>
        <ContactSidebar />
      </ErrorBoundary>
      <ErrorBoundary
        fallback={(error, reset) => (
          <div className="error-panel">
            <p>Something went wrong: {error.message}</p>
            <button onClick={reset}>Try Again</button>
          </div>
        )}
        onError={(error) => logErrorToService(error)}
      >
        <ContactDetail />
      </ErrorBoundary>
    </div>
  );
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In ContactsHub, Manoj encountered a crash where the ContactDetail component threw a TypeError: Cannot read properties of null when a contact was deleted while the detail view was still open. The contact data became null mid-render, and without an Error Boundary, the entire app went white.

Manoj added an ErrorBoundary around ContactDetail with a reset callback. But the fix wasn't complete — the ErrorBoundary caught the error and showed the fallback, but clicking "Try Again" re-rendered the same null data and crashed again immediately. The real fix required two parts: (1) the ErrorBoundary for graceful degradation, and (2) the ContactDetail component needed a null check: if (!contact) return <p>Select a contact</p>. The ErrorBoundary was the safety net; the null check was the actual fix. Lesson: Error Boundaries handle the unexpected; defensive coding handles the expected.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Error Boundaries do not catch errors in async code, event handlers, or SSRUse Result<T, E> pattern for async operations; Error Boundaries are only for synchronous render errors
Result pattern requires checking .success at every call site, creating verbosityCreate helper functions: unwrapOr(result, defaultValue), mapResult(result, fn) to reduce boilerplate
Error Boundary catches error but re-render triggers the same error (infinite loop)Provide a reset mechanism that clears the bad state; combine with a null guard in the child component
TypeScript's catch clause types errors as unknown, losing type informationUse type guards: if (error instanceof AppError) or const appErr = AppError.from(error) to restore typed error handling

7. Validation Strategy

  • Result type test: Write unit tests that verify API client returns { success: false, error: { code: 'NOT_FOUND' } } for 404 responses and { success: true, data } for 200 responses
  • Error Boundary test: Use React Testing Library to render a component that throws, verify the fallback UI appears and the reset button works
  • Exhaustiveness check: Use TypeScript's never type in a switch default to ensure all error codes are handled at compile time
  • Playwright E2E: Delete a contact while viewing its detail, verify the app shows the error fallback (not a white screen) and remains navigable

Designing a Practical Error Boundary Implementation in TS for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

A production-grade error boundary system in TypeScript for a mid-size product combines three layers: (1) a typed Result monad for all async/data operations ensuring errors are never silently ignored, (2) React Error Boundary components for UI resilience with granular fallback zones, and (3) a global error handler that captures uncaught exceptions, unhandled promise rejections, and logs them to an error tracking service. Together, these layers ensure that errors are typed, contained, recoverable, and observable.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub application, the error boundary system was designed to handle: Fastify API errors (validation, auth, DB failures), Cloudinary upload failures (timeout, quota exceeded), React rendering crashes, and offline/PWA scenarios where the service worker serves stale data. The implementation created a unified error taxonomy shared between frontend and backend, with error codes that mapped directly to user-facing messages. The React app had three ErrorBoundary zones: sidebar, main content, and image upload — so a Cloudinary failure in the image uploader didn't break the contact form.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// === Shared Error Taxonomy (used by both frontend and backend) ===
// shared/errors.ts

export const ERROR_CODES = {
  VALIDATION: 'VALIDATION',
  NOT_FOUND: 'NOT_FOUND',
  UNAUTHORIZED: 'UNAUTHORIZED',
  FORBIDDEN: 'FORBIDDEN',
  UPLOAD_FAILED: 'UPLOAD_FAILED',
  OFFLINE: 'OFFLINE',
  RATE_LIMITED: 'RATE_LIMITED',
  INTERNAL: 'INTERNAL',
} as const;

export type ErrorCode = typeof ERROR_CODES[keyof typeof ERROR_CODES];

export interface AppError {
  code: ErrorCode;
  message: string;
  field?: string;
  retryable: boolean;
}

export type Result<T> =
  | { success: true; data: T }
  | { success: false; error: AppError };

export const ok = <T>(data: T): Result<T> => ({ success: true, data });
export const fail = (error: AppError): Result<never> => ({ success: false, error });

// Map HTTP status to error code
export function httpToAppError(status: number, body: any): AppError {
  const map: Record<number, ErrorCode> = {
    400: 'VALIDATION', 401: 'UNAUTHORIZED', 403: 'FORBIDDEN',
    404: 'NOT_FOUND', 429: 'RATE_LIMITED',
  };
  return {
    code: map[status] || 'INTERNAL',
    message: body?.error?.message || 'Something went wrong',
    field: body?.error?.field,
    retryable: status === 429 || status >= 500,
  };
}


// === Layer 1: API Client with Result<T> ===
// frontend/src/api/client.ts

async function apiCall<T>(url: string, options?: RequestInit): Promise<Result<T>> {
  // Handle offline (PWA scenario)
  if (!navigator.onLine) {
    return fail({ code: 'OFFLINE', message: 'You are offline', retryable: true });
  }

  try {
    const res = await fetch(url, {
      ...options,
      headers: {
        'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        Authorization: `Bearer ${getToken()}`,
        ...options?.headers,
      },
    });

    const body = await res.json();

    if (!res.ok) {
      return fail(httpToAppError(res.status, body));
    }

    return ok(body.data ?? body);
  } catch (err) {
    return fail({
      code: 'INTERNAL',
      message: err instanceof Error ? err.message : 'Network error',
      retryable: true,
    });
  }
}

// Typed API functions
export const contactsApi = {
  list: () => apiCall<Contact[]>('/api/contacts'),
  create: (data: CreateContactDTO) =>
    apiCall<Contact>('/api/contacts', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(data) }),
  uploadAvatar: (contactId: string, file: File) => {
    const form = new FormData();
    form.append('avatar', file);
    return apiCall<{ url: string }>(`/api/contacts/${contactId}/avatar`, {
      method: 'POST',
      body: form,
      headers: {}, // Let browser set Content-Type for FormData
    });
  },
};


// === Layer 2: React Error Boundary with retry + error reporting ===
// frontend/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx

import React, { Component, ReactNode } from 'react';

interface Props {
  zone: string; // 'sidebar' | 'main' | 'upload' — for error tracking
  fallback: (error: Error, reset: () => void) => ReactNode;
  children: ReactNode;
}

interface State {
  hasError: boolean;
  error: Error | null;
}

class ErrorBoundary extends Component<Props, State> {
  state: State = { hasError: false, error: null };

  static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): State {
    return { hasError: true, error };
  }

  componentDidCatch(error: Error, info: React.ErrorInfo): void {
    // Report to error tracking with zone context
    reportError({
      error: error.message,
      stack: error.stack,
      zone: this.props.zone,
      componentStack: info.componentStack,
    });
  }

  reset = (): void => this.setState({ hasError: false, error: null });

  render(): ReactNode {
    if (this.state.hasError && this.state.error) {
      return this.props.fallback(this.state.error, this.reset);
    }
    return this.props.children;
  }
}

// === Layer 3: Global Error Handler ===
// frontend/src/errorHandler.ts

function initGlobalErrorHandler(): void {
  window.addEventListener('error', (event) => {
    reportError({
      error: event.message,
      filename: event.filename,
      line: event.lineno,
      zone: 'global',
    });
  });

  window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection', (event) => {
    reportError({
      error: event.reason?.message || String(event.reason),
      zone: 'unhandled-promise',
    });
  });
}

function reportError(payload: Record<string, any>): void {
  // Send to backend error tracking endpoint
  navigator.sendBeacon('/api/errors', JSON.stringify({
    ...payload,
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    userAgent: navigator.userAgent,
    url: window.location.href,
  }));
}


// === Putting it all together in App.tsx ===

function App() {
  return (
    <div className="app-layout">
      <ErrorBoundary zone="sidebar" fallback={(err, reset) => (
        <div className="error-fallback">
          <p>Contact list unavailable</p>
          <button onClick={reset}>Reload</button>
        </div>
      )}>
        <ContactSidebar />
      </ErrorBoundary>

      <ErrorBoundary zone="main" fallback={(err, reset) => (
        <div className="error-fallback">
          <p>{err.message}</p>
          <button onClick={reset}>Try Again</button>
        </div>
      )}>
        <ContactDetail />
      </ErrorBoundary>

      <ErrorBoundary zone="upload" fallback={(err, reset) => (
        <div className="error-fallback">
          <p>Image upload failed</p>
          <button onClick={reset}>Retry Upload</button>
        </div>
      )}>
        <AvatarUploader />
      </ErrorBoundary>
    </div>
  );
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In ContactsHub, Manoj faced a production issue where users reported a blank screen but the error tracking service showed no errors. The root cause: a Promise rejection in a useEffect cleanup function was swallowed silently. The useEffect returned a cleanup that called abortController.abort(), which caused a pending fetch to reject with AbortError. This rejection was unhandled because the .catch() handler wasn't attached to the aborted fetch promise.

The fix required three steps: (1) add the global unhandledrejection listener (Layer 3) to catch the error in the tracking service, (2) filter out AbortError from error reporting since it's expected during cleanup, and (3) ensure every fetch call in the API client checks if (err.name === 'AbortError') return before returning a failure Result. The blank screen was actually caused by a subsequent state update on the unmounted component; adding the abort check prevented the error cascade.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Unhandled promise rejections in useEffect cleanup cause silent failuresAdd global unhandledrejection listener; always .catch() promises that can be aborted; filter AbortError from error reports
Error Boundary reset re-renders child which immediately throws again (loop)Add retry count state; after 3 consecutive failures, show a permanent fallback with a "Contact support" message instead of retry button
TypeScript's unknown catch clause type makes error handling verboseCreate an AppError.from(unknown) factory that normalizes any thrown value into a typed AppError
PWA offline mode: service worker serves cached page but API calls failCheck navigator.onLine before API calls; return Result with OFFLINE code; show cached data with a banner “You are offline”

7. Validation Strategy

  • Layer 1 test: Mock fetch to return various HTTP status codes (400, 401, 404, 500); verify apiCall returns correctly typed Result with appropriate AppError codes
  • Layer 2 test: Use React Testing Library to render a component that throws during render; verify ErrorBoundary shows the fallback and the reset button clears the error state
  • Layer 3 test: Dispatch a synthetic ErrorEvent on window; verify reportError is called with the correct payload including zone information
  • Integration test: Playwright test that simulates a network failure mid-operation, verifies the app shows appropriate error UI (not a white screen) and recovers when network is restored
  • Offline test: Playwright test with network throttling to offline; verify PWA shows cached contacts with offline banner
